input.query_title: Random graphs with arbitrary degree distributions and their applications
input.query_abstract: Recent work on the structure of social networks and the internet has focussed attention on graphs with distributions of vertex degree that are significantly different from the Poisson degree distributions that have been widely studied in the past. In this paper we develop in detail the theory of random graphs with arbitrary degree distributions. In addition to simple undirected, unipartite graphs, we examine the properties of directed and bipartite graphs. Among other results, we derive exact expressions for the position of the phase transition at which a giant component first forms, the mean component size, the size of the giant component if there is one, the mean number of vertices a certain distance away from a randomly chosen vertex, and the average vertex-vertex distance within a graph. We apply our theory to some real-world graphs, including the world-wide web and collaboration graphs of scientists and Fortune 1000 company directors. We demonstrate that in some cases random graphs with appropriate distributions of vertex degree predict with surprising accuracy the behavior of the real world, while in others there is a measurable discrepancy between theory and reality, perhaps indicating the presence of additional social structure in the network that is not captured by the random graph.
input.hyp_generic_object: the onward-transmission structure of a host population with a heterogeneous, arbitrary secondary-case (offspring) distribution
input.hyp_abstract: Classical outbreak models assume each infectious host generates a Poisson-distributed number of secondary cases, yet contact-tracing data repeatedly reveal strong individual-level heterogeneity, with a minority of hosts responsible for a disproportionate share of onward transmission. We develop a general framework for outbreak dynamics in populations with an arbitrary secondary-case (offspring) distribution. Encoding this distribution in a probability generating function, and treating early spread as a branching process in which transmission chains are locally tree-like, we obtain closed-form expressions for the invasion threshold separating self-limiting clusters from self-sustaining epidemics, the probability that a single introduction ignites a major outbreak, and the expected number of cases reachable within a given number of transmission generations. Iterating the generating-function recursion yields the full distribution of minor-outbreak sizes and, above threshold, the fraction of the population ultimately infected. We extend the treatment to asymmetric transmission between demographic strata and to settings where infectiousness and susceptibility are governed by separate distributions. Applied to empirical secondary-case counts from respiratory and vector-borne pathogens, the theory reproduces observed final-size and extinction statistics with striking fidelity in some settings, whereas systematic departures in others point to correlations in contact structure not captured by the assumption of independent transmission events.
input.candidate_title: Social consensus through the influence of committed minorities
input.candidate_abstract: We show how the prevailing majority opinion in a population can be rapidly reversed by a small fraction p of randomly distributed committed agents who consistently proselytize the opposing opinion and are immune to influence. Specifically, we show that when the committed fraction grows beyond a critical value p_c \approx 10%, there is a dramatic decrease in the time, T_c, taken for the entire population to adopt the committed opinion. In particular, for complete graphs we show that when p < p_c, T_c \sim \exp(α(p)N), while for p > p_c, T_c \sim \ln N. We conclude with simulation results for Erdős-Rényi random graphs and scale-free networks which show qualitatively similar behavior.
